query:
We are deciding where to submit a conference abstract for the TMS 2027 Annual
Meeting & Exhibition (Orlando, FL, March 14-18, 2027; abstracts due July 15,
2026). The attached bundle contains:

  - abstract.md: our draft 143-word abstract, "Modular Benchtop Self-Driving
    Laboratory for Closed-Loop Aqueous Materials Chemistry Using
    Interchangeable Powder, Liquid, and Sensing Modules." It presents the
    CubXL, a modular gantry-based benchtop self-driving laboratory (SDL)
    running the open-source CubOS control layer (version-controlled YAML
    protocols, offline motion validation, local experiment database), with
    interchangeable deck modules for liquid handling, single-powder dosing via
    manually swappable cartridges, in-line pH measurement, vial
    capping/decapping, and camera-based monitoring.

  - tms2027-symposia-full-text.md (and tms2027-symposia.json): the COMPLETE
    call-for-abstracts text of ALL TMS 2027 symposia (~108), scraped from
    ProgramMaster (the same content TMS renders into each symposium's flyer
    PDF): title, sponsoring division/committee, organizers with affiliations,
    full scope text, and deadline.

NEW CONTEXT: we are strongly considering extending the platform to
ELECTROCHEMISTRY processes -- e.g., adding a potentiostat/galvanostat module
and electrode handling so the SDL can run closed-loop electrochemical
campaigns (candidate directions: electrodeposition/electroplating,
electrolyte formulation and screening, electrowinning/electrorefining at
vial scale, corrosion/passivation screening, energy-storage/battery
electrolyte or electrode screening, electrocatalysis). Nothing is built yet;
the abstract would present the platform plus a credible electrochemistry
roadmap, or a reframed electrochemistry-forward version of the current
aqueous-chemistry abstract.

Your task: recommend, ranked, the TOP 10 TMS 2027 symposia that would MOST
WELCOME an AUTONOMOUS ELECTROCHEMISTRY platform talk of this kind.

Requirements:

1. FULL-TEXT SWEEP WITH AN ELECTROCHEMISTRY LENS. Systematically consider all
   symposia in the attached set; do not shortlist by title alone. Sweep for
   electrochemistry-relevant scope language: electrodeposition, electrolytes,
   electrometallurgy, electrowinning/electrorefining, corrosion, batteries,
   energy storage/conversion, electrocatalysis, molten salt vs aqueous
   electrochemistry, sensors, and any scope inviting autonomous/high-
   throughput/closed-loop or AI/ML-guided experimentation. Explicitly flag
   HIDDEN GEMS whose titles would never be shortlisted but whose full scope
   text fits.

2. ORGANIZER BACKGROUNDS. For each candidate, look up the research
   backgrounds of the listed organizers (electrochemistry, lab automation,
   self-driving labs, high-throughput experimentation, open hardware).
   Organizer receptiveness matters as much as scope fit. Note relevant
   organizer backgrounds in each rationale.

3. FOR EACH OF THE TOP 10, report:
   - rank, symposium title, sponsoring division/committee;
   - fit score out of 10 for an electrochemistry-forward CubXL abstract;
   - 2-4 sentence rationale quoting operative phrases from the scope text;
   - organizer-background notes (who, affiliation, receptive or not, why);
   - whether it is a HIDDEN GEM;
   - which electrochemistry application framing (electrodeposition,
     electrolyte screening, corrosion, electrometallurgy, batteries, ...)
     maximizes fit for that symposium, and whether a compliant <=150-word
     abstract can credibly make that framing given nothing is built yet.

4. RISK ASSESSMENT. For the top 3, note the risk of proposing an
   electrochemistry capability that is still a roadmap item rather than a
   demonstrated result, and how to word the abstract honestly (e.g.,
   "extensible to", "we present the architecture and initial integration
   of...") without overclaiming.

5. BOTTOM LINE. The single best-fit symposium for the electrochemistry
   direction, plus two backups, and one sentence each on why. Also state
   whether the electrochemistry framing opens meaningfully better venues than
   the aqueous-chemistry framing, or whether it is better kept as one line
   inside an aqueous-chemistry abstract ("future modules include a
   potentiostat for closed-loop electrochemistry").
answer:
I swept all 108 attached TMS 2027 symposium records using the full scope text, not titles alone, for electrochemistry terms, aqueous and molten-salt processing terms, corrosion/passivation, batteries/energy storage, sensors, and autonomous/high-throughput/AI/ML/closed-loop language. I then checked organizer backgrounds by web lookup where organizer receptiveness materially affected the ranking. The ranking below is for an **electrochemistry-forward CubXL/CubOS abstract** that is honest that the potentiostat/electrode module is a roadmap or initial-integration direction, not a demonstrated capability.

## Ranked top 10

### 1. Next Generation Electrometallurgical Technologies for Metal Extraction, Refining, and Recycling

**Sponsorship:** TMS Extraction and Processing Division; TMS: Hydrometallurgy and Electrometallurgy Committee  
**Fit score:** **9.5/10**  
**Hidden gem:** No. The title already points straight at electrometallurgy.

This is the best match. The scope explicitly seeks “**electrochemical extraction, refining, and recycling of metals**,” “**advanced electrolytes**,” “**electrochemical cell design**,” “**electrolyte stability**,” “**closed-loop system design**,” “**process control**,” and “**modularization**.” A CubXL talk can be framed as a vial-scale autonomous testbed for electrolyte formulation, electrode/cell handling, electrochemical process screening, provenance capture, and closed-loop optimization before scale-up.

**Organizer-background notes:** Takanari Ouchi lists research keywords including extractive metallurgy and electrochemistry; Samira Sokhanvaran has visible electrometallurgy work from Hatch; Athanasios Karamalidis works on aqueous separations and rare-element recovery. This committee is likely to understand why benchtop electrochemical experimentation, electrolyte stability, impurity tolerance, and modular process control matter.

**Best application framing:** Electrometallurgy, electrowinning/electrorefining, electrolyte screening, recovery from e-waste/batteries/magnet scrap.  
**Can a <=150-word abstract credibly use this framing if nothing is built?** Yes, but only if worded as architecture plus roadmap or initial integration: “we present a modular SDL architecture extensible to potentiostat-controlled vial-scale electrometallurgy” rather than “we demonstrate autonomous electrowinning.”

---

### 2. AI-Enabled Materials Processing: Integrating Accelerated Experimental Workflows and Processing-Aware Machine Learning

**Sponsorship:** TMS Materials Processing and Manufacturing Division; TMS: Additive Manufacturing Committee; TMS: Computational Materials Science and Engineering Committee; TMS: Process Technology and Modeling Committee  
**Fit score:** **9.0/10**  
**Hidden gem:** **Yes.** The title does not say electrochemistry, but the scope is almost written for an SDL platform.

The scope centers on “**integration of artificial intelligence and machine learning directly with experimental materials processing**,” “**high-throughput manufacturing**,” “**real-time process monitoring**,” “**Bayesian optimization**,” “**adaptive experimentation**,” and “**closed-loop control in manufacturing-relevant environments**.” It also explicitly includes “**surface modification, and deposition-based routes**” and applications in “**coatings, batteries, and other processing-intensive systems**,” which gives room for electrodeposition, electrolyte/process screening, and electrochemical coating workflows.

**Organizer-background notes:** Sreenivas Raguraman’s profile and scholar records show machine-learning-guided accelerated structure-property discovery; Timothy Weihs is a materials processing and fabrication researcher with commercialization experience; the rest of the organizer list is strong in processing, additive manufacturing, and process-aware ML. This group should be highly receptive to the CubOS/YAML/provenance/Bayesian optimization story, even if they are not primarily electrochemists.

**Best application framing:** Autonomous electrodeposition or electrochemical surface modification as a processing route; secondarily battery/electrolyte process screening.  
**Can a <=150-word abstract credibly use this framing?** Yes. The safest abstract should emphasize the demonstrated platform architecture and say “future/initial electrochemical modules target potentiostat-controlled deposition and electrolyte screening.”

---

### 3. Rare Metal Extraction & Processing

**Sponsorship:** TMS Extraction and Processing Division; TMS: Hydrometallurgy and Electrometallurgy Committee  
**Fit score:** **8.6/10**  
**Hidden gem:** No, though the title is broader than the electrochemistry content.

The scope covers “**critical minerals**,” waste/residue/tailings/recycling feedstocks, and explicitly says topics span “**bio-, hydro-, and electro-metallurgy**.” It also invites “**externally stimulated separations (e.g., electric, magnetic, ultrasonic)**” and “**novel electrochemical separations**.” A CubXL abstract would fit best as a small-volume, closed-loop screening system for rare-metal leaching, separation chemistry, electrolyte composition, and electrochemical recovery conditions.

**Organizer-background notes:** Gisele Azimi’s lab describes expertise in electrochemistry, thermodynamics, hydrometallurgy, supercritical fluids, and strategic-material recovery; Kerstin Forsberg works on separation processes; Amilton Barbosa Botelho Junior is in hydrometallurgy and has work on recycling and electrodialysis; Hong Peng leads sustainable extraction engineering. This is a technically receptive organizer pool for automated hydrometallurgy/electrometallurgy screening.

**Best application framing:** Critical-metal extraction, electrochemical separations, electrodialysis/electrowinning, leachate/electrolyte screening.  
**Can a <=150-word abstract credibly use this framing?** Yes, if the abstract keeps electrochemistry as “planned module enabling” and anchors current capability in aqueous dosing, pH, sealed vials, provenance, and closed-loop optimization.

---

### 4. Materials and Chemistry for Molten Salt Systems

**Sponsorship:** TMS Structural Materials Division; TMS: Corrosion and Environmental Effects Committee  
**Fit score:** **8.0/10**  
**Hidden gem:** No for electrochemistry, but yes for an SDL talk if you would not normally search molten-salt symposia for lab automation.

The scope says it covers “**all aspects of materials science, chemistry, and electrochemistry in molten salt systems**” for “**energy transfer, energy storage, metallurgical processing, and actinide recovery**.” It also lists “**Corrosion of salt-facing materials**,” “**Electrochemistry for metal processing and actinide recovery**,” and “**Electrochemistry in molten salts**.” The catch: your current benchtop aqueous vial platform is not a molten-salt platform, so this becomes a stretch unless the abstract is explicitly about architectural extensibility rather than near-term experiments.

**Organizer-background notes:** Stephen Raiman works on corrosion and degradation in extreme environments, including molten salts; Karen Chen-Wiegart works in synchrotron/tomography-based materials characterization, including batteries and energy materials; the organizer list includes molten-salt, corrosion, nuclear, and materials-in-extreme-environments expertise. They would understand electrochemical control and corrosion monitoring, but they may expect high-temperature or salt-specific capability.

**Best application framing:** Molten-salt electrochemistry roadmap, corrosion/passivation screening, salt chemistry/electrochemical monitoring.  
**Can a <=150-word abstract credibly use this framing?** Only cautiously. A compliant abstract can say the CubXL architecture is being extended toward electrochemical modules relevant to molten-salt workflows, but it should not imply molten-salt handling is already feasible.

---

### 5. Advances in Recycling and Environmental Technologies

**Sponsorship:** TMS Extraction and Processing Division; TMS Light Metals Division; TMS: Energy Committee; TMS: Recycling and Environmental Technologies Committee  
**Fit score:** **7.8/10**  
**Hidden gem:** **Yes.** The title sounds broad, but the full scope directly mentions electrohydrometallurgy and AI optimization.

The scope invites “**hydrometallurgical, electrohydrometallurgical and pyrometallurgical processes to obtain critical metals from secondary sources**,” “**separation of metals in water-based systems**,” and “**AI-based modeling for recycling process optimization**.” This is a very good aqueous-chemistry-to-electrochemistry bridge. CubXL could be pitched as an accessible automated platform for screening leaching/separation/electrohydrometallurgical variables under closed-loop optimization.

**Organizer-background notes:** Adam Powell works on metal process development for clean energy and energy efficiency; Hong Peng leads sustainable extraction engineering; Amilton Barbosa Botelho Junior works in hydrometallurgy and recycling; several organizers overlap with critical-metal extraction and recovery communities. Receptiveness should be good if the abstract emphasizes recycling process screening rather than generic automation.

**Best application framing:** Recycling, electrohydrometallurgy, critical-metal recovery from secondary sources, aqueous separation screening.  
**Can a <=150-word abstract credibly use this framing?** Yes. This can be honest even without the electrochemistry module if you frame current CubXL capabilities as aqueous sample-prep and pH-controlled screening, with the potentiostat as an extension.

---

### 6. Environmental Degradation of High-Temperature Alloys, Multiple Principal Component Materials, and Coatings

**Sponsorship:** TMS Structural Materials Division; TMS: High Temperature Alloys Committee; TMS: Corrosion and Environmental Effects Committee  
**Fit score:** **7.5/10**  
**Hidden gem:** **Yes.** The title suggests alloy degradation, but the full scope explicitly welcomes high-throughput testing and aqueous/molten-salt corrosion.

The scope includes “**oxidation, corrosion (aqueous, high-temperature, molten salt, liquid metal)**,” “**in-situ monitoring**,” “**high-throughput testing**,” “**data-driven materials design**,” and “**machine learning approaches**.” A CubXL talk can fit as an autonomous corrosion/passivation screening platform: controlled aqueous chemistries, pH, time, imaging, electrode/potentiostat roadmap, and closed-loop selection of test conditions.

**Organizer-background notes:** Paraskevas Kontis, Stephen Raiman-adjacent corrosion communities, Chantal Sudbrack, Stoichko Antonov, Bronislava Gorr, Mark Weaver, and Xiaolei Guo point to strong oxidation/corrosion/environmental-degradation expertise. This audience may value throughput and in situ monitoring, but they will expect corrosion-relevant measurements; pH and camera monitoring alone may be viewed as preliminary.

**Best application framing:** Corrosion/passivation screening, aqueous corrosion chemistry, high-throughput environmental exposure, future electrochemical impedance/potentiodynamic testing.  
**Can a <=150-word abstract credibly use this framing?** Yes, if phrased as “architecture for autonomous aqueous exposure and planned potentiostat-based corrosion modules,” not as demonstrated electrochemical corrosion testing.

---

### 7. Circular Metallurgy: Design, Technology, Application

**Sponsorship:** TMS Functional Materials Division; TMS Structural Materials Division; TMS: Alloy Phases Committee  
**Fit score:** **7.2/10**  
**Hidden gem:** **Yes.** The title would not obviously catch an electrochemistry platform search, but the scope explicitly names lab automation and robotics.

The scope is about sustainable production and recycling, with themes including “**AI and machine learning approaches for sustainable materials discovery and process optimization**,” “**closed-loop metallurgical systems**,” and “**scale up circularity through lab automation, robotics, process optimization and AI/ML**.” This is less electrochemistry-specific than the extraction symposia, but very receptive to an SDL platform if framed as infrastructure for circular-metallurgy process development.

**Organizer-background notes:** Bin Ouyang has visible work on autonomous synthesis laboratories and materials discovery; Wei Xiong’s lab lists high-throughput experimentation, machine learning, CALPHAD/ICME, and materials informatics; Alan Luo works on circular aluminum alloy design. Organizer receptiveness to automation/AI is high, while electrochemistry specificity is moderate.

**Best application framing:** Closed-loop circular-metallurgy process screening, recycling flowsheets, aqueous leaching plus future electrochemical recovery.  
**Can a <=150-word abstract credibly use this framing?** Yes. This is one of the safest “platform plus roadmap” venues because the full scope explicitly welcomes lab automation/robotics.

---

### 8. Energy Technology 2027: Advancement in Energy Materials - Theory, Simulation, Characterization and Application

**Sponsorship:** TMS Extraction and Processing Division; TMS: Energy Committee; TMS: Process Technology and Modeling Committee  
**Fit score:** **7.0/10**  
**Hidden gem:** Somewhat. The title is broad, but the scope gives useful hooks for electrochemical energy materials.

The scope includes “**energy generation, storage, and conversion**,” “**development and application of advanced materials for energy storage and energy conversion**,” “**in-situ/ex-situ**” characterization, and “**data-driven design and machine learning in energy materials**.” The strongest CubXL framing would be battery/electrolyte or electrocatalysis screening, but the current platform has not yet demonstrated electrochemical testing, so the fit is more aspirational.

**Organizer-background notes:** Eric Detsi’s scholar profile lists electrochemical energy conversion/storage, electrochemical dilatometry, solvent decomposition, and battery-related work; Donna Guillen works in modeling/simulation and energy systems; other organizers span energy materials, extraction, and process modeling. Receptiveness is good for energy electrochemistry, but the abstract needs a real application hook.

**Best application framing:** Battery electrolyte screening, electrocatalysis, energy-storage material testing, electrochemical energy conversion.  
**Can a <=150-word abstract credibly use this framing?** Borderline but possible. It should say “designed to support future potentiostat-coupled electrolyte screening” unless you can build even a minimal potentiostat integration before submission.

---

### 9. Functional Nanomaterials 2027

**Sponsorship:** TMS Functional Materials Division; TMS: Nanomaterials Committee  
**Fit score:** **6.8/10**  
**Hidden gem:** **Yes.** The title does not advertise electrochemistry, but the scope explicitly advertises autonomous experiments.

The scope lists applications including “**energy generation and storage**” and “**advanced sensors**,” and says the symposium will focus on nanomaterial design “**advanced by the use of AI and autonomy**.” It also promises focused sessions on strategies “**aided by AI and autonomous experiments**,” including “**Data-Driven and Autonomous Discovery in Nanomaterials**.” This is a good venue only if the electrochemistry direction is tied to nanomaterial synthesis, electrodeposition of nanostructures, sensors, or energy-storage nanomaterials.

**Organizer-background notes:** Keerti Kappagantula’s PNNL page describes nanotechnology plus machine learning/artificial intelligence/data-driven methods; Mostafa Bedewy develops in situ/operando methodologies for nanomaterials fabrication and has ML-linked nanomanufacturing work. Organizer receptiveness to autonomous experimentation is high, but the electrochemistry audience depth is uncertain.

**Best application framing:** Electrodeposition of functional nanomaterials, nanostructured electrodes, electrochemical sensors, battery/supercapacitor nanomaterials.  
**Can a <=150-word abstract credibly use this framing?** Only if the abstract foregrounds autonomous nanomaterials workflows as future targets. It is weaker if presented as general aqueous chemistry.

---

### 10. Artificial Intelligence Applications in Integrated Computational Materials Engineering (AI-ICME)

**Sponsorship:** TMS Materials Processing and Manufacturing Division; TMS: Computational Materials Science and Engineering Committee; TMS: Integrated Computational Materials Engineering Committee; TMS: Process Technology and Modeling Committee  
**Fit score:** **6.6/10**  
**Hidden gem:** **Yes.** It is not electrochemistry-specific, but it directly names autonomous experimentation.

The scope welcomes “**active learning, Bayesian optimization, and autonomous/closed-loop experimentation for accelerated discovery and processing optimization**,” plus “**data curation, standards, provenance/traceability, and reproducible machine learning operations**.” Those are exact CubOS strengths: YAML protocols, offline validation, local database, provenance, and closed-loop optimization. The weakness is that this symposium is ICME-heavy; electrochemistry must be positioned as one application of a general process-structure-property optimization infrastructure.

**Organizer-background notes:** Brandon Bocklund is known for CALPHAD tools and thermodynamic modeling infrastructure; Pinar Acar works on optimization, uncertainty quantification, reduced-order modeling, and ML; Ram Devanathan and other organizers bring computational/materials-modeling depth. They are likely receptive to provenance, validation, and closed-loop design, but may expect stronger model integration than the current abstract shows.

**Best application framing:** Closed-loop experimental data generation and provenance for electrochemical process optimization; link to Bayesian optimization and ICME-style process-structure-property models.  
**Can a <=150-word abstract credibly use this framing?** Yes, but only if the abstract emphasizes data/provenance/model-guided workflow rather than hardware novelty alone.

## Honorable mentions and exclusions

- **AI/ML/Data Informatics for Materials Discovery: Bridging Experiment, Theory, and Modeling** was close. It welcomes “FAIR data,” provenance, “agentic AI,” and methods connecting “laboratory practice” with AI-assisted materials development. I would use it as a backup only if the abstract is rewritten around CubOS data infrastructure and autonomous decision-making, not electrochemistry.
- **Printed Electronics and Additive Manufacturing of Advanced Materials, Devices and Functional Constructs** has hooks for “batteries and supercapacitors,” sensors, robotics, and predictive ink/process control. It is weaker unless you add a direct-write/electrode/printed-device angle.
- **Hume-Rothery Symposium: Data-Driven Materials Discovery and Phase Stability** has excellent language, including “autonomous and closed-loop materials discovery frameworks,” but the attached scope says **“This symposium only accepts invited abstracts.”** I would not treat it as a realistic submission target unless you have an invitation.

## Roadmap-risk assessment for the top 3

### 1. Next Generation Electrometallurgical Technologies

**Risk:** Moderate. The scope is ideal, but the audience will know electrochemical cells, electrodes, electrolytes, impurity tolerance, and current/potential control. If no potentiostat module exists by July 2026, an abstract that sounds like demonstrated electrowinning/electrorefining would overclaim.

**Honest wording:** “We present a modular SDL architecture for aqueous materials chemistry and outline an electrochemistry module under integration, coupling electrode handling and potentiostat control to closed-loop electrolyte and process screening.” Avoid “we demonstrate autonomous electrowinning” unless you actually have it.

### 2. AI-Enabled Materials Processing

**Risk:** Low to moderate. This symposium values workflow architecture, closed-loop control, and accelerated experimental processing, so a platform talk can stand even if electrochemistry is an extension. The risk is weaker electrochemistry specificity, not overclaiming.

**Honest wording:** “We demonstrate the platform’s current liquid/powder/sensing modules and describe how the same CubOS protocol and provenance layer extends to potentiostat-controlled electrodeposition and electrolyte-screening workflows.”

### 3. Rare Metal Extraction & Processing

**Risk:** Moderate. Current CubXL capabilities already fit aqueous leaching/separation studies, but not electrochemical separation unless a potentiostat/electrode module is built or at least credibly prototyped. The proceedings/journal note also means the work may need more technical substance than a pure roadmap abstract.

**Honest wording:** “Current modules support automated aqueous leaching and pH-controlled screening; planned electrochemical modules will enable vial-scale electrodialysis/electrowinning parameter studies with full experimental provenance.”

## Bottom line

**Single best-fit symposium:** **Next Generation Electrometallurgical Technologies for Metal Extraction, Refining, and Recycling**. It is the only venue where electrochemical extraction/refining/recycling, advanced electrolytes, closed-loop design, process control, and modularization all appear together in the scope.

**Backup 1:** **AI-Enabled Materials Processing: Integrating Accelerated Experimental Workflows and Processing-Aware Machine Learning**. It is the best SDL/closed-loop/AI venue and can accept electrodeposition or electrochemical surface modification as a processing route.

**Backup 2:** **Rare Metal Extraction & Processing**. It is the best aqueous-to-electrochemical extraction venue after the electrometallurgy symposium, especially for rare-metal recovery, electrochemical separations, and hydrometallurgical screening.

The electrochemistry framing **does open meaningfully better venues** than the current aqueous-chemistry framing, especially the Hydrometallurgy and Electrometallurgy Committee symposia. But unless a minimal potentiostat/electrode integration can be built before submission, I would not make the whole abstract sound like a completed electrochemistry SDL. Best practical move: submit an electrometallurgy-forward version that presents demonstrated CubXL/CubOS aqueous automation, then honestly says future or initial modules include potentiostat-controlled electrode handling for closed-loop electrochemical process screening.

## Discretionary decisions

- Ranked symposia by combined scope fit and organizer receptiveness, not by keyword count alone.
- Weighted explicit electrochemistry/electrometallurgy language more strongly than generic AI/ML language.
- Included AI/automation symposia as hidden gems when their full scope explicitly welcomed closed-loop, autonomous, high-throughput, Bayesian-optimization, or lab-automation workflows.
- Penalized symposia where electrochemistry was only tangential, even if they were strong for SDLs generally.
- Excluded the Hume-Rothery symposium from the top 10 despite strong autonomous-discovery language because the attached scope states that it accepts invited abstracts only.
- Treated the potentiostat/electrode module as a roadmap item because the prompt states that nothing is built yet; all recommended framings avoid claims of demonstrated autonomous electrochemistry.
- Used a 0-10 qualitative fit score based on the attached TMS scope text plus organizer-background web lookups, rather than a formal statistical model.

import json, re, pandas as pd
from pathlib import Path
syms=json.loads(Path('/workspace/tms2027-symposia.json').read_text())
print(len(syms))
rows=[]
terms = ['electrochem','electrodeposition','electroplating','electrolyte','electrowinning','electrorefining','corrosion','passivation','battery','batteries','energy storage','energy conversion','electrocatal','molten salt','aqueous','sensor','autonomous','high-throughput','machine learning','artificial intelligence','closed-loop','automation','robot','optimization','data-driven','computational','in situ','operando']
for i,s in enumerate(syms):
    text=(s['title']+'\n'+s.get('scope','')).lower()
    hits={t: len(re.findall(re.escape(t), text)) for t in terms}
    score=sum(hits.values())
    rows.append({'idx':i,'title':s['title'],'sponsorship':'; '.join(s.get('sponsorship',[])),'score':score, **hits})
df=pd.DataFrame(rows).sort_values('score', ascending=False)
print(df[['idx','title','score','electrochem','electrolyte','corrosion','battery','batteries','molten salt','aqueous','machine learning','artificial intelligence','high-throughput','autonomous']].head(40).to_string(index=False))

cells.source:
# Display candidate symposia containing electrochem-related or AI/high-throughput language with excerpts around hits
query_terms = ['electrochem','electrodeposition','electroplating','electrolyte','electrowinning','electrorefining','corrosion','passivation','battery','batteries','energy storage','energy conversion','electrocatal','molten salt','aqueous','high-throughput','autonomous','closed-loop','automation','robot']
for i,s in enumerate(syms):
    text=s['scope']
    low=(s['title']+' '+text).lower()
    if any(t in low for t in query_terms):
        hits=[t for t in query_terms if t in low]
        print('\n###', i, s['title'])
        print('Sponsorship:', '; '.join(s.get('sponsorship',[])))
        print('Hits:', hits)
        print('Organizers:', '; '.join([o['name']+' ('+o['affiliation']+')' for o in s.get('organizers',[])]))
        # Print selected bullet lines containing terms + first scope paragraph
        lines=[ln.strip() for ln in text.splitlines() if ln.strip()]
        print('First:', lines[0][:500] if lines else '')
        for ln in lines:
            if any(t in ln.lower() for t in query_terms):
                print('  HIT:', ln[:700])

First: This symposium provides a platform for researchers, engineers, and scientists to discuss their latest research findings on the mechanical behavior of multi-principal element alloys (MPEAs).
  HIT: BACKGROUND AND RATIONALE: MPEAs are alloys that are composed of multiple primary elements and can consist of face-centered-cubic (FCC), body-center-cubic (BCC), or hexagonal-close-packed (HCP) solid-solution phases. Recent research has shown that these alloys exhibit desirable properties, such as high strength and ductility, high fatigue/wear resistance, good corrosion resistance, and notable irradiation resistance. These desirable attributes, therefore, make MPEAs suitable for a wide range of applications in the automotive, aerospace, and biomedical industries.
  HIT: • Novel uses of in situ and high-throughput characterization techniques, such as neutron and high energy X-ray diffraction,  three-dimensional atom probe tomography, transmission electron microscopy, and electron backscatter diffraction

This symposium centers on the integration of artificial intelligence and machine learning directly with experimental materials processing, rather than on general AI-driven materials discovery. While many current AI and ML efforts emphasize composition or purely computational modeling, comp
  HIT: Processing governs microstructure evolution, defect populations, and interfaces across metals, alloys, composites, and functional materials, thereby determining reliability, manufacturability, and performance. This symposium centers on the integration of artificial intelligence and machine learning directly with experimental materials processing, rather than on general AI-driven materials discovery. While many current AI and ML efforts emphasize composition or purely computational modeling, comparatively less attention has been given to treating processing history as a primary design variable. Advances in high-throughput manufacturing, accelerated characterization and testing, and real-time 
  HIT: The symposium focuses on process-aware microstructure and property�control enabled by AI integrated with thermomechanical processing, heat treatment, casting, additive manufacturing, surface modification, and deposition-based routes. Contributions may be experimental, computational, or hybrid in nature, but should emphasize coupling AI with processing science to accelerate optimization and scale-up. Particular emphasis is placed on generating and structuring high-quality processing-microstructure-property datasets from manufacturing workflows and leveraging them to encode processing histories into predictive models of microstructure and properties. Topics include AI-assisted exploration of c
  HIT: Bayesian optimization, adaptive experimentation, and closed-loop control in manufacturing-relevant environments
  HIT: Applications in structural alloys, biocompatible materials, additively manufactured components, coatings, batteries, and other processing-intensive systems

This symposium invites abstracts on the development and application of novel algorithms for materials science and engineering.

    s=syms[i]
    print('\n'+'='*100)

    print('SCOPE:\n', s['scope'][:3500])

====================================================================================================
IDX 82: Next Generation Electrometallurgical Technologies for Metal Extraction, Refining, and Recycling
SPONSORSHIP: TMS Extraction and Processing Division; TMS: Hydrometallurgy and Electrometallurgy Committee
ORGANIZERS: Samira Sokhanvaran (Hatch); Takanari Ouchi (Waseda University); Rich Bradshaw , Worcester Polytechnic Institute (WPI) (Athanasios Karamalidis); Pennsylvania State University ()
SCOPE:
 Electrometallurgy is emerging as a critical pillar of modern metal production, driven by global priorities in decarbonization, electrification, and secure critical mineral supply chains. This symposium will highlight recent scientific and industrial advancements in electrochemical extraction, refining, and recycling of metals, with a particular focus on innovations that enable fossil-free and energy-efficient metallurgical operations. Topics will cover the development of advanced electrolytes, improvements in electrochemical cell design, and the expansion of electrometallurgical methods into new metal systems, including oxides, sulfides, and complex secondary resources.
The symposium will also emphasize pathways to recover critical and strategic minerals from electronic waste, batteries, and magnet scrap, showcasing emerging technologies that improve selectivity, scalability, and environmental performance. Additional focus areas include the integration of green hydrogen, CO₂-to-materials electrochemistry, and hybrid flowsheets that pair electrochemical processes with membrane, solvent extraction, or thermal operations.
Environmental and lifecycle considerations remain central to the symposium’s objectives. Contributions addressing off-gas management, electrolyte stability, closed-loop system design, and techno-economic assessment are encouraged. Finally, the symposium seeks to bridge fundamental research with industrial application by examining the challenges of scaling from laboratory experiments to pilot facilities and full industrial deployment. Lessons learned from recent demonstration projects will provide insight into process control, impurity tolerance, modularization, and regulatory constraints.
This symposium aims to define the next generation of electrometallurgical technologies and provide a platform for researchers, industry practitioners, and technology developers to exchange knowledge and accelerate commercialization.
1.	Electrometallurgy for decarbonization and the energy transition.
2.	Advanced electrolytes and next-generation electrochemical cell designs.
3.	Electrochemical extraction of oxides, sulfides, and complex ores.
4.	Innovations in inert electrodes, high-amperage systems, and process intensification.
5.	Production of high-purity and critical metals for advanced applications.
6.	Recovery of strategic elements from e-waste, batteries, and magnet scrap.
7.	CO₂-conversion and green-hydrogen-enabled electrochemical reduction pathways.
8.	Environmental challenges, lifecycle assessment, and closed-loop process design.
9.	Scale-up challenges from lab to pilot to industrial operations.

====================================================================================================
IDX 95: Rare Metal Extraction & Processing
SPONSORSHIP: TMS Extraction and Processing Division; TMS: Hydrometallurgy and Electrometallurgy Committee
ORGANIZERS: Michael J. Dziekan (Argonne National Laboratory); Athanasios Karamalidis (Pennsylvania State University); Kerstin M. Forsberg (KTH Royal Institute of Technology); Kurniawan Kurniawan (Helmholtz Institute Freiberg for Resource Technology (HIF), Helmholtz-Zentrum Dresden - Rossendorf (HZDR)); Amilton Barbosa Botelho Junior (Norwegian University of Science and Technology); Jaeheon Lee (Colorado School of Mines); Jihye Kim (Colorado School of Mines); Gisele Azimi (University of Toronto); Hong Peng (University of Queensland); Shafiq Alam (University of Saskatchewan); Neale R. Neelameggham (IND LLC); Alafara Abdullahi Baba (University of Ilorin); Anne-Marie Suriano (Rio Tinto); Takanari Ouchi (Waseda University)
SCOPE:
 This symposium will cover extraction of critical minerals with a focus on critical and rare metals, from both conventional and unconventional feedstocks. Unconventional feedstocks include waste materials and residues, recycling of critical elements, tailings, and extraction processing byproducts resultant from metal production.
The critical minerals and rare metals of interest include antimony, bismuth, barium, beryllium, boron, calcium, chromium, gallium, germanium, hafnium, indium, lithium, magnesium, manganese, molybdenum, niobium, platinum group metals, rare earth metals, rhenium, scandium, selenium, silicon, sodium, strontium, tantalum, tellurium, tungsten, and other elements not covered by other TMS symposia.
Critical element extraction and processing topics will span bio-, hydro-, and electro-metallurgy for recovering rare metals from both conventional and unconventional feedstocks (e.g., residues, recycled materials, tailings, and metal production process streams). Novel processes such as biomimicking leaching and separation, externally stimulated separations (e.g., electric, magnetic, ultrasonic), in-situ recovery, solar-thermal reaction synthesis, novel electrochemical separations, and cold crucible synthesis of rare metals will be considered. Processing and refining studies for critical minerals and rare metals spanning laboratory, pilot-scale, and industrial applications will be included.
Note regarding publication: Authors seeking an oral or poster presentation opportunity must submit a manuscript for the proceedings or be accepted for publication in a TMS journal.

IDX 73: Materials and Chemistry for Molten Salt Systems
SPONSORSHIP: TMS Structural Materials Division; TMS: Corrosion and Environmental Effects Committee
ORGANIZERS: Stephen Raiman (University of Michigan); Yu-Chen Karen Chen-Wiegart (Stony Brook University/ Brookhaven National Lab.); Amanda Leong (Virginia Polytechnic Institute); Guiqiu T. Zheng (Commonwealth Fusion Systems); Weiyue Zhou (Massachusetts Institute of Technology); Kumar Sridharan (University of Wisconsin-Madison); James C. Earthman (University of California-Irvine); Sangtae Kim (Hanyang University)
SCOPE:
 The use of molten salts as a coolant in molten salt reactors (MSR) and concentrating solar power (CSP) systems offers many advantages including low operating pressures, high temperatures, and favorable heat transfer. Molten salts are also widely used in the metal processing and nuclear fuels reprocessing industries.  Despite the advantages, the highly aggressive molten salts present a challenging environment for salt facing materials.
This symposium covers all aspects of materials science, chemistry, and electrochemistry in molten salt systems for diverse purposes such as energy transfer, energy storage, metallurgical processing, and actinide recovery.
Abstracts are solicited in, but not limited to, the following topics:
Corrosion of salt-facing materials
Salt effects in graphite and moderator materials
Radiation effects in structural, moderator, and fuel materials
Alloy selection and design for molten salt applications
Interaction of fission products with materials
Novel materials for use in molten salt systems
Mechanical and creep properties
Electrochemistry for metal processing and actinide recovery
Salt chemistry effects on materials including radiolysis
Heat exchanger design
Welding and cladding issues
Waste handling and actinide recovery
Electrochemistry in molten salts
Salt property evaluation and calculation

====================================================================================================
IDX 21: AI-Enabled Materials Processing: Integrating Accelerated Experimental Workflows and Processing-Aware Machine Learning
SPONSORSHIP: TMS Materials Processing and Manufacturing Division; TMS: Additive Manufacturing Committee; TMS: Computational Materials Science and Engineering Committee; TMS: Process Technology and Modeling Committee
ORGANIZERS: Sreenivas Raguraman (Johns Hopkins University); Maitreyee Sharma Priyadarshini (Virginia Tech); Timothy P. Weihs (Johns Hopkins University); Thomas Voisin (Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory); Allison M. Beese (Pennsylvania State University); Samantha Webster (Colorado School of Mines)
SCOPE:
 Processing governs microstructure evolution, defect populations, and interfaces across metals, alloys, composites, and functional materials, thereby determining reliability, manufacturability, and performance. This symposium centers on the integration of artificial intelligence and machine learning directly with experimental materials processing, rather than on general AI-driven materials discovery. While many current AI and ML efforts emphasize composition or purely computational modeling, comparatively less attention has been given to treating processing history as a primary design variable. Advances in high-throughput manufacturing, accelerated characterization and testing, and real-time process monitoring now create opportunities to embed data-driven and physics-informed methods within experimental processing workflows.
The symposium focuses on process-aware microstructure and property�control enabled by AI integrated with thermomechanical processing, heat treatment, casting, additive manufacturing, surface modification, and deposition-based routes. Contributions may be experimental, computational, or hybrid in nature, but should emphasize coupling AI with processing science to accelerate optimization and scale-up. Particular emphasis is placed on generating and structuring high-quality processing-microstructure-property datasets from manufacturing workflows and leveraging them to encode processing histories into predictive models of microstructure and properties. Topics include AI-assisted exploration of complex parameter spaces for processing; surrogate and reduced-order models for complex processing routes; uncertainty-aware and few-shot learning approaches for small or sparse datasets; Bayesian optimization for process tuning; and closed-loop experimental workflows that integrate synthesis, processing, characterization, testing, and iterative model-guided refinement.
The goal of this symposium is to bring together materials processors, metallurgists, manufacturing engineers, and data scientists working at the interface of experimental processing and data-driven optimization. By positioning processing as a central axis in AI-enabled materials development, this symposium aims to accelerate the translation of laboratory-scale insights into robust, manufacturable, and scalable materials systems.
Topics of Interest Include, But Are Not Limited To:
Integration of AI/ML with experimental processing for microstructure and property control, process optimization, and manufacturing scale-up
High throughput and data-aware experimental design for systematic exploration of processing parameter spaces
Processing-structure-property relationships supported by advanced and�accelerated characterization, testing, and real-time process monitoring
Bayesian optimization, adaptive experimentation, and closed-loop control in manufacturing-relevant environments
Applications in structural alloys, biocompatible materials, additively manufactured components, coatings, batteries, and other processing-intensive systems
Experimental advances in casting, rolling, extrusion, forging, heat treatment, additive manufacturing, and deposition-based processes

====================================================================================================
IDX 68: Hume-Rothery Symposium: Data-Driven Materials Discovery and Phase Stability
SPONSORSHIP: TMS Functional Materials Division; TMS Structural Materials Division; TMS Materials Processing and Manufacturing Division; TMS: Alloy Phases Committee; TMS: Integrated Computational Materials Engineering Committee
ORGANIZERS: Bi-Cheng Zhou (University of Virginia); Bin Ouyang (Vanderbilt University); Wei Chen (University at Buffalo); James Edward Saal (Citrine Informatics); Vidvuds Ozolins (Yale University)
SCOPE:
 The past two decades have witnessed transformative advances in computational materials science, driven by the convergence of first-principles calculations, thermodynamic modeling, high-throughput computation, and more recently, machine learning and artificial intelligence. Together, these approaches have enabled unprecedented predictive capability in identifying stable and metastable compounds, resolving complex phase equilibria, and accelerating the discovery of novel materials.
Prof. Chris Wolverton has been a pioneering force in this transformation. His contributions to high-throughput density functional theory, data-driven materials design, and the integration of machine learning with thermodynamics and electronic-structure theory have profoundly expanded our ability to predict novel materials and elucidate structure–property relationships, cementing his role as one of the defining figures in modern computational materials science.
This Hume-Rothery Symposium honors Prof. Wolverton's contributions by convening leading experts in theory, computation, data science, and experiments to assess the state of the art in computational and data-driven materials discovery, and to chart emerging directions enabled by machine learning and AI.
Topics will include, but are not limited to:
• First-principles prediction of phase stability and phase diagrams
• High-throughput computational materials discovery
• Machine learning and artificial intelligence for materials design
• Data-driven approaches to phase stability and thermodynamics
• Integration of CALPHAD, first-principles calculations, and ML methods
• Prediction and discovery of functional materials
• Computational design of alloys and complex material systems
• Thermodynamic modeling and metastability
• Materials databases and informatics infrastructure for accelerated discovery
• Experimental validation of computationally predicted materials
• Autonomous and closed-loop materials discovery frameworks
Note: This symposium only accepts invited abstracts.

====================================================================================================
IDX 58: Environmental Degradation of High-Temperature Alloys, Multiple Principal Component Materials, and Coatings
SPONSORSHIP: TMS Structural Materials Division; TMS: High Temperature Alloys Committee; TMS: Corrosion and Environmental Effects Committee
ORGANIZERS: Paraskevas Kontis (Federal Institute for Materials Research and Testing (BAM)); Wenbo Wang (Oakland University); Katerina A. Christofidou (University of Sheffield); Steffen Neumeier (Friedrich-Alexander-Universit�t Erlangen-N�rnberg); Chantal K. Sudbrack (National Energy Technology Laboratory); Stoichko Antonov (National Energy Technology Laboratory); Luciana Maria Bortoluci Ormastroni (Safran Aircraft Engines); Satoshi Utada (National Institute for Materials Science); Karthikeyan Hariharan (Indian Institute of Technology, Bombay); Bronislava Gorr (Karlsruhe Institute of Technology (KIT)); Junliang Liu (Florida State University); Mark L. Weaver (University of Alabama); XiaoXiang Yu (Novelis Inc.); Xiaolei Guo (Colorado School of Mines)
SCOPE:
 This symposium focuses on environmental degradation in advanced material systems, including high-temperature alloys, multi-principal element alloys (MPEAs), high-entropy alloys (HEAs), high-entropy ceramics (HECs), and advanced coatings. These materials are essential for applications in extreme environments such as aerospace propulsion, power generation, and industrial processing. As service conditions become increasingly aggressive, degradation phenomena—including oxidation, corrosion, hydrogen embrittlement, and complex environment-material interactions—pose significant challenges. Understanding these mechanisms is critical for predicting component lifetimes and enabling the design of next-generation materials.
This symposium will provide a forum for researchers and engineers to discuss advances in experimental evaluation, multi-scale modeling, innovative mitigation strategies, novel coating architectures and data-driven materials design, for lifetime prediction. Emphasis will be placed on bridging experimental observations with computational predictions, linking environmental conditions to degradation mechanisms, and identifying microstructural features governing resistance. Contributions spanning fundamental studies, applied research, and industrial case analyses are encouraged. Contributions exploring in-situ monitoring, high-throughput testing, and correlative approaches are particularly encouraged. The session aims to integrate perspectives from academia, national laboratories, and industry to address current challenges and emerging opportunities.
Themes of interest include, but are not limited to:
Mechanisms of Environmental Attack:
oxidation, corrosion (aqueous, high-temperature, molten salt, liquid metal), hydrogen effects and degradation, and radiation-assisted damage and cracking.
Advanced Characterization:
Correlative, multi-modal microscopy and spectroscopy.
Multiscale Modeling & Computational Simulation:
ICME, thermodynamic-kinetic modelling, multi-scale simulations of degradation processes.
Testing Methodologies:
In-situ monitoring, complex environment simulation (e.g., steam, supercritical CO
2
), high-throughput testing; coupled mechanical–environmental degradation (e.g. stress-corrosion cracking, corrosion fatigue).
Microstructure–Property Relationships:
phase stability, defect evolution, and degradation-induced property changes.
Coatings & Surface Engineering:
Novel coating architectures, diffusion barriers, and microstructural evolution during service.
Alloy Design & Lifetime Prediction:
Materials optimization strategies for enhancing environmental resistance, data-driven design, machine learning approaches, and physics-based models for remaining useful life assessment.

IDX 17: Advances in Recycling and Environmental Technologies
SPONSORSHIP: TMS Extraction and Processing Division; TMS Light Metals Division; TMS: Energy Committee; TMS: Recycling and Environmental Technologies Committee
ORGANIZERS: Adam C. Powell (Worcester Polytechnic Institute); Hong Peng (University of Queensland); Amilton Barbosa Botelho Junior (Norwegian University of Science and Technology); Jihye Kim (Colorado School of Mines); Mert Efe (Pacific Northwest National Laboratory); Yixi Tian (Massachusetts Institute of Technology); Shafiq Alam (University of Saskatchewan); Kurniawan Kurniawan (Helmholtz Institute Freiberg for Resource Technology (HIF), Helmholtz-Zentrum Dresden - Rossendorf (HZDR)); Xiaodong Ma (University Of Queensland); Elif Emil Kaya (Norwegian University of Science and Technology); Fiseha Tesfaye (Metso Metals Oy); Thomas P. Battle ()
SCOPE:
 This topic encompasses innovative approaches to recycling and resource recovery, aiming to address challenges towards sustainable mining at a near-to-waste generation.
This symposium includes technologies (but not restricted to):
Hydrometallurgical, electrohydrometallurgical and pyrometallurgical processes to obtain critical metals from secondary sources;
Bio-based process for the recovery of critical metals from urban�and mining�waste;
Fundamental understanding of separation of metals in water-based systems;�
Recovery of metals from metallurgical slags;
AI-based modeling for recycling process optimization
Projects evaluating economic viability and environmental impact of Recycling processes, while the establishment of technical standards ensures quality, safety, and harmonized practices across regions�are motivated.

IDX 57: Energy Technology 2027: Advancement in Energy Materials - Theory, Simulation, Characterization and Application
SPONSORSHIP: TMS Extraction and Processing Division; TMS: Energy Committee; TMS: Process Technology and Modeling Committee
ORGANIZERS: Wenwen Song (University of Kassel); Donna P. Guillen (Idaho National Laboratory); Fiseha Tesfaye (Metso Metals Oy); Shafiq Alam (University of Saskatchewan); Eric Detsi (University of Pennsylvania); Hong Peng (University of Queensland); Tuan A.H. Nguyen (University of Queensland); Duhan Zhang (Massachusetts Institute of Technology); Lei Zhang (University of Alaska Fairbanks)
SCOPE:
 With the increasing global focus on transitioning to sustainable and efficient energy systems, energy materials have become a foundation for enabling advanced technologies for renewable energy, e.g. hydrogen, biofuels, wind, solar. This symposium focuses on recent breakthroughs in the development, understanding, and application of energy materials across diverse fields, including energy generation, storage, and conversion. Topics of interest include fundamental research on the design and optimization of energy materials through computational and theoretical approaches, multi-scale modelling, and advanced characterization techniques. This symposium aims to bring together a multidisciplinary community of researchers and engineers working on the cutting edge of energy materials. It will provide a collaborative platform for sharing knowledge, exchanging innovative ideas, and discussing methodologies to tackle current challenges in the field. By fostering dialogue across theory, experiment, and application, the symposium seeks to inspire novel approaches to accelerate advancements in energy materials for a sustainable future.
Exemplary topics at the symposium include but are not limited to:
•	Theoretical approaches for energy materials design, including density functional theory (DFT), molecular dynamics (MD), and thermodynamic modeling, to predict the properties and behavior of energy materials. Insights into atomic structures, defect energetics, and phase stability enable the design of materials tailored for specific energy applications, such as hydrogen, biofuels.
•	Multiscale simulations for energy materials design and property prediction, covering quantum, atomistic, mesoscale, and continuum levels.
•	Advanced characterization techniques for energy materials, including in-situ/ex-situ synchrotron X-ray diffraction, in-situ/ex-situ neutron scattering, microscopic analysis, atom probe tomography etc., which reveals micro- to nano scale features and dynamic structural and chemical changes during energy generation, storage, and conversion processes.
•	Development of functional energy materials. This topic focuses on the development and application of advanced materials for energy storage and energy conversion. Emphasis is placed on optimizing material properties, including conductivity, stability, and efficiency, for integration into sustainable energy technologies.
•	Interface and surface engineering in energy materials, e.g. materials science technology to modify interface characteristics, segregation control, coating technology, to enhance energy efficiency and materials durability.
•	Data-driven design and machine learning in energy materials. This topic highlights the use of data-driven approaches and machine-learned interatomic potentials to analyze experimental and simulation data, predict material properties, and explore to develop next-generation energy materials.
•	Applications of energy materials in sustainable technologies. Analysis related to material sustainability, such as circularity measurements, life cycle assessment, etc.
Note regarding publication: Authors seeking an oral presentation opportunity must submit a manuscript for the Energy Technology proceedings or be accepted for publication in a TMS journal.
